caption:
Every time a brand says "limited time only," a conversion dies somewhere.

Not because urgency doesn't work; it does. But because shoppers have been trained to ignore urgency that feels fake. "We sell out fast!" after restocking three times doesn't build pressure. It builds distrust.

Anchoring creates real psychological pressure. "Limit 3 per customer" implies demand without saying it. Specificity does what vague urgency never can.

X X people bought this in the the last 24 hours We sell out fast! •Overstock sale! Only available through [specific date] You're getting [value] for just [price] You're getting [value] for just [price]'.

caption:
Humans are funny things…🤔

Often the simplest circumstance can have powerful after effects. (The key is to understand WHEN these emotions come up, not so much why.)
Don't try to outsmart your customer, just meet them where they are.

caption: The side quest usually ends in a purchase. Make sure you're part of it.
firstComment: The purchase journey is rarely linear. Someone sees your ad, goes to Reddit, then YouTube, then back to your site, then checks a competitor, then buys. Being present in those side quests — review sites, comparison content, UGC — is where brands lose or win the conversion without ever knowing it.

latestComments.text: The purchase journey is rarely linear. Someone sees your ad, goes to Reddit, then YouTube, then back to your site, then checks a competitor, then buys. caption: Price anchoring. Affect heuristic. Familiarity bias. Scarcity effect. Endearment. Framing. These are the six levers that the smartest DTC brands I've worked with pull (without touching their budget) and they consistently see 20–40% drops in CPM and CPA as a result.

It's underperforming because you're ignoring psychology. The algorithm doesn't reward pretty ads. It rewards ads that make people feel something and act.'.

caption:
Your customer didn't forget about you. They just have a cat.

The real buyer journey isn't a straight line ,it's 18 chaotic steps that look nothing like the marketing funnel you were sold in school. Ads. Organic. Email. Cart abandonment. Late-night doom scroll. Influencer mention. The friend who casually brought it up at brunch.

Save this and send it to every brand owner who thinks one ad should "just work."

alt: Photo by Sarah Levinger on March 18, 2026. May be a Twitter screenshot of text.

caption:
The unsubscribe wasn't about the discount, it was about the pressure.

Most brands treat urgency like a volume knob. 

The lower the conversions, the louder they turn it up. But psychology tells us the opposite is true: push too hard, too fast, and people don't buy... they leave.
firstComment: Urgency works when it's anchored in a real constraint the customer already accepts. When it's manufactured it reads as desperation and the customer feels that viscerally. The pressure signal is often louder than the offer itself.

latestComments.text: Urgency works when it's anchored in a real constraint the customer already accepts. When it's manufactured it reads as desperation and the customer feels that viscerally. The pressure signal is often louder than the offer itself.

caption: Do you understand what they believe? What they're afraid of? What made them finally say yes? That's your golden ticket.

alt: Photo by Sarah Levinger on March 09, 2026. May be an image of rat, rodent, hamster and text that says 'Staunch belief of mine: the hamster wheel of making ads will never stop if you continue to believe extra effort !! More ads. More variations. More copy angles. More creative. More budget. better results. And somehow, still not enough. The problem isn't your output. It's the assumption underneath it. The brands that get off the hamster wheel aren't the ones working harder. They're the ones who actually understand their customer. With that, one ad can do what fifty couldn't. Effort without insight is just a faster hamster wheel.'.

Photo by Sarah Levinger on March 08, 2026. May be a graphic of card, magazine, poster and text that says 'Brands know their customer feels overwhelmed, Or insecure, Or stuck. They build the messaging around it and wonder why it's not landing. Why? Timing is Everything. A parent doesn't feel the weight of not being present every hour of the day. It usually hits on a Sunday night when the week is about to start again. Someone unhappy with their finances doesn't feel it constantly. It hits when the bill arrives, or when a friend mentions a vacation they can't afford. The feeling is . But it's triggered by a specific moment.'.

Photo by Sarah Levinger on March 08, 2026. May be a graphic of ‎poster and ‎text that says '‎Your ad hitting חס a random Tuesday afternoon is competing with a brain that isn't in that emotional state yet. The same ad, served at the right moment, feels like it was made specifically for them. Know what your customer feels, then do the harder work of figuring when they feel it. out That's where the timing advantage lives.‎'‎‎.

caption: The difference isn't the offer, it's the trust sitting behind it.
alt: Photo by Sarah Levinger on March 10, 2026. May be a graphic of poster, magazine and text that says 'Regardless of what you've been told, it's not the offer that drives behavior, it's the belief in the offer that drives behavior. childPosts.alt: Photo by Sarah Levinger on March 10, 2026. May be a graphic of poster, magazine and text that says 'Regardless of what you've been told, it's not the offer that drives behavior, it's the belief in the offer that drives behavior. Photo by Sarah Levinger on March 10, 2026. May be a graphic of poster, magazine and text that says 'Psychologists gists call this accumulated trust. People don't evaluate offers in isolation. They evaluate them through the lens of everything they already know and feel about you. This is why a brand with a loyal audience can send one email and sell out in hours, while a brand with no relationship history can run the same deal for a week and hear nothing. If your offers aren't converting, the problem probably isn't the offer... it's what people believe about you before they even read it. Fix that first. 大'.

caption: PSA: growth comes when you stop interpreting the problem and start experiencing it with them.

caption:
Start smaller than you think you need to.
That's not weakness. That's strategy. 🧠
alt: Photo by Sarah Levinger on March 07, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'knows: Science now Small commitments made over a short period of time lead us to make larger commitments over a long period of time. This is called the foot-in-the-door effect and it's one of the most powerful forces shaping who you become. Your brain is always watching what you do, not just what you say. Every small act of follow-through quietly tells your nervous system: this is who we are now.'.

caption: One good headline change can outperform a hundred random tests. So I'm leaning away from the "run more ads" game this year. Comment 🔥 if you're with me.
alt: Photo by Sarah Levinger on March 05, 2026. Moral of the story: skip the random tests. Go for emotional resonance instead.'.

caption: A simple 🦕 can be the difference-maker for your brand recognition. Do you have a "signature" emoji you use in your marketing? Drop it in the comments! 👇 (Then read the full study here: https://app.sciencesays.com/p/how-to-use-emojis-effectively)

Photo by Sarah Levinger on March 07, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'The Von Restorff effect: The thing that stands out from its surroundings is the thing that gets remembered. An inbox full of subject lines looks like noise. A dinosaur in the middle of that noise is a pattern interrupt. When you use the same emoji consistently, your audience stops just noticing it. They start associating it with you you specifically, before they even read your name. Pick something that means something to you. Use it everywhere. Let Let it become yours.'.

caption: Their comments! Their questions! Their language! Their objections! That's where the real magic lives.
alt: Photo by Sarah Levinger on March 08, 2026. May be an image of magazine, poster and text that says 'PSA: copying your competitor's: ads won't boost your performance. It just leads you in circles. What you're seeing is the the surface: The creative. The hook. The format. What you're not seeing is: the brand equity behind it, the audience they spent years building trust with, and the 50 failed tests that came before it. Copying the output without understanding the input never works. Stop reverse engineering your competitors. Start engineering for your customer.'.

caption: Variable rewards are the secret to long-term LTV (they're the reason why gameshows still exist!). Pro tip: if you're using gamification in your business (mystery gifts, quizzes, etc) keep your rewards random and rotating to keep the brain engaged and coming back for more. 🎾
alt: Photo by Sarah Levinger on March 06, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'How to build an offer that will get your customers psychologically hooked on your brand: 1. Choose a reward people want. 2. Randomly deliver the reward. 3. Rotate the reward every every 2-4 weeks. Sporadically delivering rewards tells the brain to EEP AT AT IT which boosts LTV.'.
